title: Thickened dye paste for resist printing
subtitle: Using sodium alginate paste to apply plant dye to fibres in controlled areas
excerpt: Natural dye pastes for surface application require a thickening agent to hold the colour in place during fixing. Sodium alginate, a seaweed-derived gel, is the standard choice: it dissolves in water, carries the dye liquor, and rinses clean after steam-setting. Results are softer and more muted than synthetic reactive dyes, which is characteristic of the natural-dye palette.
sourceNotes:
- Jenny Dean, Wild Color: surface application of natural dyes, alginate thickeners
- Dominique Cardon, Natural Dyes: printing pastes, steaming for fixation on protein fibres
- Maiwa Handprints technical notes: sodium alginate stock preparation, dye concentrate ratios
- General practice: weld and madder concentrate preparation for paste printing

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural thickening agent derived from brown seaweed; used in dye printing as a carrier gel that holds pigment or dye in place and rinses clean after fixing.
A metallic salt (such as alum or iron) used to chemically bond dye to fibre, improving both colour uptake and washfastness.
The process of exposing dyed or printed fabric to steam heat to fix the dye permanently into the fibre structure.
A strong, reduced-volume dye liquor produced by simmering a large quantity of dyestuff in a small amount of water; used in printing pastes where liquid volume must be kept low.
Animal-derived fibre such as wool, alpaca, or silk; takes natural dye with mordant more readily than plant-based (cellulose) fibres.

content.text: Printing natural dye requires a paste that holds the colour in place and does not bleed before fixing.
Sodium alginate
is the standard thickener. It makes a stable, paintable gel that carries the
dye concentrate
without reacting with it and rinses out cleanly after
steam setting
. This works best on
protein fibres
(wool, silk) that have been pre-
mordanted
with alum. Madder and weld concentrates give the best results for paste printing: both produce strong, workable colours in the natural-dye range.
Wear gloves throughout. Keep dye vessels separate from food use. Steam fixing generates hot vapour: use oven mitts and lift lids away from you.
Method

Step 1: Pre-mordant the fabric. Dissolve alum at 15% weight of fibre in warm water; add pre-wetted wool or silk fabric. Raise to 80°C, hold for 45 minutes. Cool and remove. The fabric may be dried and stored for several days before printing.

Step 2: Prepare a 6% sodium alginate stock gel. Weigh 6 g of sodium alginate powder per 100 ml of cold water. Sprinkle the powder onto the surface of the water while stirring constantly with a whisk; do not add all at once. Continue stirring for 3-5 minutes until the gel begins to thicken. Cover and leave to hydrate for 1-2 hours or overnight. The stock keeps refrigerated for up to 2 weeks.

Step 3: Prepare a strong dye concentrate. Simmer your dye material (madder root or weld tops) at 100-150% weight of fibre in a small amount of water (just enough to cover) for 1 hour at 80°C. Strain and reduce the liquor by gentle simmering until the volume is approximately 50-60 ml per 100 g of fabric. The concentrate should be deep in colour.

Step 4: Mix the dye paste. Combine 2 parts alginate stock gel with 1 part dye concentrate and stir until fully blended. The paste should be thick enough to hold a brushstroke edge without bleeding. If it flows too freely, add more gel; if it is too stiff to apply, add a few drops of dye concentrate.

Step 5: Apply the paste. Lay the pre-mordanted fabric flat on a clean surface. Apply the dye paste using a foam brush, stencil and squeegee, or block print. Work quickly and do not over-apply: a thin, even coat fixes better than a thick, wet layer.
Step 6: Allow the paste to dry fully before steam-fixing. Dry at room temperature for 30-60 minutes or with a low-heat hair dryer.

Step 7: Steam-set for 20 minutes. Roll the fabric loosely in clean muslin, place in a steamer basket over boiling water, and steam at approximately 100°C. Ensure the fabric does not touch the water or the steamer walls. Keep the water topped up during steaming.

Step 8: Rinse thoroughly in cool water until the water runs clear; this removes the alginate and any unfixed dye. Do not use hot water for the first rinse, as it can cause the alginate to re-gel in the fabric. Dry flat away from direct sunlight.
Variations

Stencil printing: secure a cut stencil to the dry fabric with masking tape. Apply paste through the stencil with a squeegee or stiff brush using downward strokes rather than sideways ones to minimise bleed under the stencil edge. Lift the stencil while the paste is still wet.

Multiple colours: prepare separate paste batches for each colour (madder for red-orange, weld for yellow). Apply one colour and allow it to dry before applying the next; colours can overlap for mixed-hue areas.
Troubleshooter

The paste consistency is too thin, the fabric was too damp when paste was applied, or too much paste was used. Increase the alginate ratio slightly, ensure fabric is dry before printing, and apply in thinner coats.

attrs.problem: Colour is faint after steaming and rinsing

content.text: The dye concentrate was too weak, mordanting was insufficient, or the steam-setting time was too short. Use a longer simmering time to produce a darker concentrate, verify mordant percentage, and extend steaming to 25-30 minutes.

attrs.problem: Alginate leaves a stiff residue in the fabric

content.text: The first rinse water was too hot, causing the alginate to re-gel. Soak the fabric in cool water for 20-30 minutes, then rinse repeatedly. A brief soak in diluted white vinegar (1 tablespoon per litre) can help break down residual gel.
Wastewater disposal

Spent alginate paste and alum-based dye rinse water can go down a foul drain. Alginate is food-safe and biodegrades readily; small quantities can be composted. The dye concentrate preparation rinse water (alum mordant) follows the same guidance as a standard dye bath.
